stem: A 29-year-old nulligravid woman presents with 7 months of secondary amenorrhoea, intermittent bilateral galactorrhoea and reduced libido. She has had no recent pregnancy or breastfeeding, no headaches and no visual symptoms. She started risperidone 4 mg daily 9 months ago for chronic schizoaffective disorder, prescribed by her psychiatrist, and is responding well with no recent admissions. She takes no other regular medications and uses no recreational drugs. BMI 24, BP 116/70. Visual fields are full to confrontation. Bloods: beta-hCG negative, repeat mid-morning serum prolactin 1900 mIU/L (reference <500) on two occasions one week apart, TSH and free T4 normal, U+Es normal. She is keen to conceive in the next 12 months.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral expressible galactorrhoea, no breast mass, visual fields normal to confrontation
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative, prolactin 1850 mIU/L (reference <500), TSH 1.4 mIU/L, free T4 normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Request urgent MRI pituitary with gadolinium before any further investigation
options.rationale: Urgent MRI first is wrong because when a known dopamine antagonist (risperidone) is the strongly suspected cause, identifying and managing the drug effect should precede imaging; pituitary MRI is reserved for sustained unexplained hyperprolactinaemia off the offending drug. Would be correct only after switching to a prolactin-sparing agent confirms persistence of hyperprolactinaemia (Endocrine Society 2011).

options.text: Liaise with the treating psychiatrist about switching to a prolactin-sparing antipsychotic such as aripiprazole, then re-check prolactin
options.rationale: Risperidone is a strong dopamine D2 antagonist and a common cause of pharmacological hyperprolactinaemia. The first step is collaborative medication review with the prescribing psychiatrist; switching to a prolactin-sparing agent (aripiprazole) and re-measuring before imaging avoids unnecessary MRI and identifies the cause (Endocrine Society 2011; eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Commence cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly empirically and reassess prolactin in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Empirical cabergoline is wrong because dopamine agonists can precipitate or worsen psychosis in patients with schizophrenia and are not first-line when a drug cause is likely; the anti-pattern is treating the lab abnormality without addressing the cause. Would be correct only for prolactinoma confirmed on imaging in stable patients (Endocrine Society 2011).

options.text: Macroprolactin screen with polyethylene glycol precipitation as the next test
options.rationale: Macroprolactin screen is wrong because macroprolactin testing is for asymptomatic hyperprolactinaemia; this woman is clinically symptomatic (amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, low libido), confirming biologically active prolactin. Would be correct only in asymptomatic elevation (Endocrine Society 2011).

options.text: Stop risperidone abruptly without psychiatric consultation to allow prolactin to normalise
options.rationale: Abrupt risperidone cessation is wrong because stopping antipsychotic without psychiatric input risks relapse and self-harm; switches require shared decision-making with the prescribing team. Would never be correct unilaterally (Endocrine Society 2011).

explanation.summary: Mild-to-moderate hyperprolactinaemia (typically <2000 mIU/L) should be confirmed with a repeat morning sample after excluding pregnancy, hypothyroidism, renal failure and dopamine-blocking medications (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, antidepressants); pituitary MRI follows only after secondary causes are excluded (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).
key_points: Common secondary causes: pregnancy, hypothyroidism, dopamine antagonists, oestrogens, opioids, renal failure, chest wall stimulation
Prolactin >5000 mIU/L strongly suggests prolactinoma
Macroprolactin screen if asymptomatic and isolated lab finding
MRI pituitary if confirmed persistent elevation with secondary causes excluded
explanation.pearls: Always check medications: risperidone, haloperidol, metoclopramide, SSRIs and TCAs can all cause hyperprolactinaemia.

stem: A 32-year-old woman is reviewed 8 days after IVF oocyte retrieval (21 oocytes). She reports 48 hours of worsening abdominal distension, nausea and reduced oral intake. BP 108/64, HR 108, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air. Abdomen distended, tense ascites palpable to the costal margin, mild generalised tenderness without peritonism. Pelvic ultrasound: ovaries 11 cm right and 10 cm left with multiple cysts; large-volume ascites extending above the umbilicus; small right pleural effusion. Bloods: haematocrit 0.48 (baseline 0.38), WCC 13.2, sodium 132, potassium 4.6, creatinine 96 micromol/L (baseline 62), albumin 27 g/L, ALT 78 U/L, urine output 25 mL/h for the past 4 hours.

data_table.Examination: Distended abdomen, shifting dullness, mild tenderness, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Hct 0.45 (baseline 0.38), WCC 12.5, Na 137, K 4.2, Cr 72 micromol/L, Alb 32 g/L; USS: ovaries 10 cm R / 9 cm L, moderate ascites pelvic + lower abdo, not above umbilicus

lead_in: Which classification and management plan best fit her ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome?

options.text: Moderate OHSS: discharge home with daily phone review and oral fluids
options.rationale: Moderate OHSS labelling is wrong because the haematocrit >=0.45, rising creatinine, oliguria, hypoalbuminaemia and ascites above the umbilicus push her into the severe category; outpatient management would miss thromboembolic and renal complications. Would be correct only with mild ascites and no biochemical derangement (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

options.text: Severe OHSS: admit for IV crystalloid, VTE prophylaxis with LMWH, strict fluid balance and consider paracentesis
options.rationale: Haematocrit 0.48, oliguria, creatinine rise, hypoalbuminaemia, ascites above the umbilicus and pleural effusion meet severe OHSS criteria. Management is admission for IV fluids, prophylactic LMWH (high VTE risk), strict input/output and paracentesis for symptomatic tense ascites (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

options.text: Critical OHSS: transfer to ICU now for invasive ventilation and renal replacement
options.rationale: Critical OHSS labelling is wrong because critical OHSS requires anuria, ARDS, thromboembolism or multiorgan failure; she has oliguria not anuria and normal oxygenation. Would be correct only at the critical end of the spectrum, not now (RANZCOG OHSS).

options.text: Severe OHSS: admit but give large-volume normal saline boluses until haematocrit normalises
options.rationale: Large-volume saline boluses is wrong because aggressive crystalloid worsens third-spacing and ascites in OHSS; the anti-pattern is treating it as straightforward hypovolaemia. Would be correct in haemorrhagic shock, not OHSS (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

options.text: Severe OHSS: admit but withhold thromboprophylaxis until haemoconcentration resolves
options.rationale: Withholding thromboprophylaxis is wrong because OHSS is markedly hypercoagulable (raised oestrogen, haemoconcentration, immobility) and early LMWH is recommended; the anti-pattern is treating bleeding risk and clotting risk symmetrically. Would never be correct in severe OHSS (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).

explanation.summary: OHSS severity is classified mild/moderate/severe/critical. Moderate OHSS adds sonographic ascites to mild features. Severe OHSS requires clinical ascites with haematocrit >0.45, WCC >15, hypoalbuminaemia and oliguria. Critical adds tense ascites, ARDS, VTE or renal failure (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE).
key_points: Risk factors: young age, low BMI, PCOS, high antral follicle count, high AMH, >20 oocytes retrieved
Prevention: GnRH antagonist protocol + GnRH agonist trigger + freeze-all embryos
Severe/critical OHSS = admission, VTE prophylaxis, fluid balance, paracentesis if tense
Most cases resolve by next menses; symptoms can be prolonged if pregnancy occurs
explanation.pearls: OHSS adds an independent high VTE risk; thromboprophylaxis is mandatory in admitted moderate-to-severe disease.

stem: A 38-year-old woman and her 40-year-old male partner have been trying to conceive for 18 months. The woman has regular ovulatory cycles, mid-luteal progesterone confirms ovulation, AMH 6 pmol/L (low for age), antral follicle count 6, TSH and prolactin normal, BMI 24, HyCoSy confirmed bilateral tubal patency. The partner has had two normal semen analyses by WHO 2021 criteria 4 weeks apart. Neither has had pelvic surgery, endometriosis or STI. They are non-smokers and drink within recommended limits. They have not had any prior pregnancies. They ask which ART pathway gives the highest live-birth chance given her age and ovarian reserve.

data_table.Investigations: Female: regular ovulatory cycles, normal mid-luteal progesterone, AMH 18 pmol/L, AFC 14, normal TSH/prolactin, bilateral tubal patency. Male: normal semen analysis x2 by WHO 2021

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue timed intercourse for a further 12 months before referral to a fertility specialist
options.rationale: Another 12 months of timed intercourse is wrong because women >=35 with infertility >=6-12 months warrant referral now; spending another year erodes cumulative live-birth probability, especially with low AMH. Would be correct only in women under 35 still inside the 12-month diagnostic window (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: Proceed to in vitro fertilisation given her age and low ovarian reserve
options.rationale: At 38 with AMH 6 pmol/L and AFC 6 (low ovarian reserve), IVF gives the highest cycle live-birth chance and allows aneuploidy management; IUI is less effective in this context (NICE CG156; RANZCOG).

options.text: Three cycles of intrauterine insemination with clomiphene before considering IVF
options.rationale: Three IUI cycles is wrong because IUI offers minimal incremental benefit over expectant management in unexplained infertility with low ovarian reserve at 38, and cycles spent on IUI reduce her usable IVF window. Would be correct only for mild male factor or cervical factor infertility with adequate reserve and younger age (NICE CG156).

options.text: Commence clomiphene citrate alone with timed intercourse for six cycles
options.rationale: Clomiphene alone is wrong because she is already ovulating; clomiphene targets anovulation and does not address her age-related and reserve-related decline. Would be correct only in WHO-II anovulation (e.g. PCOS) (NICE CG156).

options.text: Recommend donor oocyte IVF as her age and reserve preclude autologous IVF success
options.rationale: Donor oocyte IVF as first line is wrong because at 38 with AMH 6, autologous IVF still has reasonable cumulative live-birth probability; donor oocytes are reserved for very low reserve plus repeated autologous failure or POI. Would be correct only after failed autologous IVF or POI (RANZCOG; NICE).

explanation.summary: For unexplained infertility in a woman under 36 with normal investigations and short duration, expectant management with timed intercourse is appropriate. Stimulated IUI is considered if pregnancy has not occurred after a further 6 months, and IVF is offered for prolonged unexplained infertility, age >=36, or after failed IUI cycles (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).
key_points: Investigate at 12 months if female <35; at 6 months if female >=35; earlier if known cause
IUI is first ART step for mild male factor or ovulatory disorder responsive to mild stimulation; offers little benefit in unexplained infertility with normal semen
IVF first-line if: bilateral tubal disease, severe male factor, failed IUI, age >=40, advanced endometriosis
Single embryo transfer minimises multiple pregnancy risk
explanation.pearls: The female partner's age is the single strongest predictor of ART outcome; don't delay referral past 35.

stem: A 36-year-old woman and her 38-year-old male partner attend the GP after 8 months of regular unprotected intercourse without conception. She has had regular 28 to 30 day cycles since menarche, no dysmenorrhoea, no abnormal bleeding and no pelvic pain. She has had no pregnancies and her partner has no children from previous relationships. Her past history includes appendicectomy at age 16 without complications. She has had no pelvic infections, no chlamydia exposure, and her cervical screening is up to date. Her partner has no chronic disease, takes no regular medications, is a non-smoker and works as a teacher. They have intercourse 2 to 3 times per week. BMI 23 (female) and 25 (male). She is on folic acid 0.5 mg daily. Pelvic examination is unremarkable. They are aware of her age and ask what investigations should be commenced now rather than waiting to the conventional 12-month threshold for younger couples.

data_table.Examination: Female pelvic exam unremarkable; both partners systemically well

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the couple and review in 4 months if there is no conception
options.rationale: Reassure and review in 4 months is wrong because infertility is defined as 12 months in women <35 or 6 months in women >=35; this 36-year-old has crossed that threshold and ovarian reserve declines steeply through this age band. Would be correct only in a woman <35 within the 12-month window (RANZCOG; NICE CG156; ASRM).

options.text: Commence parallel investigation: semen analysis, mid-luteal progesterone, ovarian reserve and tubal patency
options.rationale: At >=35 years, investigation is recommended after 6 months of unprotected intercourse; investigation is parallel in both partners and includes semen analysis (WHO 2021), ovulation assessment, ovarian reserve (AMH + AFC), thyroid, prolactin and tubal patency assessment by HyCoSy or HSG (RANZCOG; NICE CG156; ASRM).

options.text: Order AMH and pelvic ultrasound only and defer semen analysis until a second visit
options.rationale: AMH and ultrasound only, deferring semen analysis, is wrong because male factor accounts for 30-40% of infertility and semen analysis is the single most informative test; deferring delays diagnosis. Would never be correct in initial workup (RANZCOG; ASRM).

options.text: Refer immediately for IVF without baseline investigations given her age
options.rationale: Immediate IVF without baseline tests is wrong because baseline tests identify treatable causes (anovulation, tubal blockage, male factor) and guide stimulation protocol choice; IVF without a semen analysis can miss severe male factor needing ICSI. Would be correct only when baseline workup has been completed and IVF chosen (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy first to exclude endometriosis as a contributing factor
options.rationale: Diagnostic laparoscopy first is wrong because laparoscopy is not first-line workup; non-invasive tests (semen analysis, hormones, HyCoSy/HSG) come first. Would be correct only with strong suspicion of endometriosis or adhesive disease after non-invasive workup (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Infertility is defined as failure to conceive after 12 months in women <35 (or 6 months in women >=35) of regular unprotected intercourse. Workup is parallel in both partners: semen analysis (WHO 2021 criteria), ovulation assessment (mid-luteal progesterone), ovarian reserve (AMH + AFC), TSH and prolactin, and tubal patency assessment by HyCoSy or HSG (RANZCOG; NICE CG156; ASRM).
key_points: Earlier workup if known anovulation, prior pelvic surgery, chemo, endometriosis, varicocele, cryptorchidism
Semen analysis: 2 samples, 4 weeks apart, after 2 to 7 days abstinence
AMH and AFC predict response to ovarian stimulation, NOT spontaneous conception
Day-2 to day-5 FSH and oestradiol are an alternative ovarian reserve marker
explanation.pearls: 30 to 40% of infertility has a male factor component; never defer semen analysis.

subtopic_detail: Infertility - couple workup timing and content

stem: A 34-year-old nulligravid woman is being assessed prior to commencement of IVF for tubal factor infertility (bilateral tubal occlusion confirmed on HyCoSy and laparoscopy). She has regular 28-day cycles with confirmed ovulation, BMI 22, normal TSH and prolactin, and her partner has a normal semen analysis. Her AMH is 4 pmol/L (low for age) and her antral follicle count is 5 (total both ovaries). Day-3 FSH is 11 IU/L with oestradiol 180 pmol/L. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic surgery beyond diagnostic laparoscopy, no autoimmune disease and no family history of premature ovarian insufficiency. She is anxious about the implications of these results and asks what they mean for her IVF cycle planning and her chance of natural conception. She has no menopausal symptoms and her BP today is normal. She is anxious about the implications for her treatment and asks specifically what these numbers mean for her IVF stimulation response.

data_table.Investigations: AMH 4 pmol/L (low for age), AFC 5 (total), Day-3 FSH 11 IU/L, oestradiol 180 pmol/L; normal TSH and prolactin; bilateral tubal occlusion

lead_in: Which statement most accurately interprets her ovarian reserve markers in the context of planning IVF?

options.text: Low AMH and AFC predict poor ovarian response and low oocyte yield, not per-cycle spontaneous conception
options.rationale: AMH and AFC are the best validated markers of ovarian response to controlled ovarian stimulation in IVF (low values predict poor response and high values predict OHSS risk); they do NOT reliably predict natural conception probability, time to menopause, or live-birth rate per oocyte (ESHRE POSEIDON; RANZCOG).

options.text: AMH below 5 pmol/L confirms premature ovarian insufficiency, so IVF with her own oocytes is inappropriate
options.rationale: Saying AMH <5 confirms POI is wrong because POI requires amenorrhoea or oligomenorrhoea PLUS FSH >25 IU/L on two readings 4 weeks apart in a woman <40; AMH alone does not diagnose POI. Would be correct only after biochemical and clinical POI criteria are met (ESHRE POI Guideline; AMS).

options.text: Her elevated day-3 FSH means she will achieve a high oocyte yield on standard stimulation protocols
options.rationale: High FSH predicting high yield is wrong because elevated day-3 FSH reflects reduced reserve and predicts poor response to stimulation; the relationship is inverse. Would never be correct (ESHRE POSEIDON; RANZCOG).

options.text: AMH and AFC accurately predict the absolute number of years remaining until menopause for an individual
options.rationale: AMH predicting years to menopause is wrong because AMH correlates with biological ovarian age at a population level but cannot reliably predict an individual's menopause timing; clinical predictors must be integrated. Would be correct only as population-level prediction, not individual counselling (ESHRE POI Guideline; SREI).

options.text: An antral follicle count above 4 excludes POSEIDON poor-responder classification regardless of the AMH
options.rationale: AFC >4 excluding POSEIDON poor-responder is wrong because POSEIDON integrates age, AMH, AFC and previous stimulation response; AFC of 5 with AMH 4 can still fall into poor-prognosis categories. Would never single-handedly exclude POSEIDON classification (ESHRE POSEIDON).

explanation.summary: AMH and AFC are the best validated predictors of ovarian response to controlled ovarian stimulation. Low values predict poor response (consider higher gonadotrophin doses, dual stimulation or oocyte accumulation strategies). High values predict OHSS risk. Neither reliably predicts spontaneous conception, time to menopause or live-birth rate per oocyte (ESHRE POSEIDON; RANZCOG).
key_points: Day-3 FSH >10 IU/L with oestradiol <200 pmol/L = diminished reserve; high E2 can mask FSH
AMH <7 pmol/L = low reserve; >35 pmol/L = high (PCOS-like) with OHSS risk
AFC <7 (total both ovaries) = poor responder
POSEIDON criteria integrate age + reserve markers + prior cycle response
explanation.pearls: Don't extrapolate from AMH to spontaneous fertility - many women with low AMH conceive naturally.

subtopic_detail: Ovarian reserve - AMH and AFC interpretation

stem: A 37-year-old woman presents to her GP after 8 months of unprotected intercourse without conception. Her partner has had a normal semen analysis. Her cycles are 26 to 28 days and she has confirmed ovulation on mid-luteal progesterone. The GP arranges day-3 cycle bloods which return as follows: FSH 9 IU/L, LH 5 IU/L, oestradiol 350 pmol/L (high), AMH 6 pmol/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, prolactin within reference range. AFC is 7 total. She has no abnormal bleeding, no significant dysmenorrhoea. Pelvic ultrasound shows a normal-sized uterus, no fibroids, normal-appearing ovaries with the AFC noted, and no endometrioma. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic surgeryse. She asks how to interpret the day-3 FSH given that it appears reassuring. Her BMI is 23 and she is otherwise well, with no symptoms suggesting menopause and

data_table.Investigations: Day-3 FSH 9 IU/L, LH 5 IU/L, oestradiol 350 pmol/L (high), AMH 6 pmol/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, prolactin normal, AFC 7

lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation of these ovarian reserve markers?

options.text: Day-3 FSH within reference range reliably excludes diminished ovarian reserve in this patient
options.rationale: Saying normal day-3 FSH excludes diminished reserve is wrong because high day-3 oestradiol (>250-290 pmol/L) suppresses FSH by negative feedback and falsely normalises the level; FSH cannot be interpreted in isolation. Would be correct only with confirmed low/normal day-3 oestradiol (ESHRE POSEIDON; RANZCOG).

options.text: Elevated day-3 oestradiol may falsely suppress FSH; reserve is better assessed by AMH and AFC here
options.rationale: Day-3 oestradiol >250 pmol/L suppresses FSH and renders the FSH uninterpretable; the AMH (6 pmol/L, low) and AFC (7, low) both indicate diminished ovarian reserve and should drive counselling and ART planning (ESHRE POSEIDON; RANZCOG).

options.text: Her LH-to-FSH ratio of less than 1 confirms PCOS as the dominant cause of her subfertility
options.rationale: LH:FSH ratio confirming PCOS is wrong because LH:FSH ratios were dropped from PCOS diagnostic criteria; PCOS is diagnosed by Rotterdam criteria (oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on ultrasound or raised AMH). Would never confirm PCOS alone (2023 International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: An AMH of 6 pmol/L with AFC of 7 confirms premature ovarian insufficiency, excluding IVF with own oocytes
options.rationale: Confirming POI is wrong because POI requires FSH >25 IU/L on two readings 4 weeks apart plus menstrual disturbance in a woman <40; low AMH/AFC alone is diminished reserve, not POI. Would be correct only with biochemical and menstrual POI criteria met (ESHRE POI Guideline; AMS).

options.text: Repeating day-3 bloods next cycle without changing strategy will give a definitive answer
options.rationale: Repeat day-3 bloods without changing strategy is wrong because the high oestradiol is the interpretive problem; AMH (cycle-independent) and AFC are the appropriate markers regardless of cycle day. Would be correct only after timing or technique is corrected (ESHRE POSEIDON).

explanation.summary: On day-3 cycle bloods, a high oestradiol (>250 to 290 pmol/L) suppresses FSH via negative feedback and can mask diminished ovarian reserve. AMH (not cycle-dependent) and AFC are more reliable in this setting; both low here, indicating diminished reserve and warranting prompt referral (ESHRE POSEIDON; RANZCOG).
key_points: Day-3 FSH must be interpreted alongside oestradiol
High oestradiol with apparently normal FSH = uninterpretable; reserve still may be low
AMH is the most reliable single ovarian reserve marker; not cycle-dependent
AFC adds prognostic information and should be measured early follicular
explanation.pearls: A 'normal' day-3 FSH with high oestradiol can be falsely reassuring.

stem: A 25-year-old nulligravid woman has just been diagnosed with PCOS by her GP (Rotterdam criteria: oligomenorrhoea, biochemical hyperandrogenism with elevated free androgen index, polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound with 24 follicles per ovary). Her exclusion bloods (TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP, beta-hCG) were all normal. She has acne, mild hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey 9) and recent 5 kg weight gain over 12 months. BMI 29. Family history: mother with type 2 diabetes diagnosed at 52, paternal uncle with myocardial infarction at age 60. She is not planning pregnancy in the next 2 years. Her GP plans the recommended metabolic screen at diagnosis per the 2023 international PCOS guideline. Blood pressure today is 124/78 mmHg. She has no symptoms of obstructive sleep apnoea, no chest pain and no peripheral oedema, and her cardiovascular examination today is unremarkable. The GP is using the 2023 international evidence-based PCOS guideline as the framework for baseline assessment.

data_table.Examination: Acne, mild hirsutism (FG 9), no acanthosis nigricans
data_table.Investigations: TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP normal; beta-hCG negative; free androgen index elevated; TVUS 24 follicles/ovary

lead_in: Which combination of investigations represents the most appropriate metabolic screen at the time of PCOS diagnosis in this woman?

options.text: Fasting lipid panel and a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test plus blood pressure measurement
options.rationale: The 2023 international PCOS guideline recommends a 75 g OGTT (preferred over fasting glucose or HbA1c due to higher sensitivity in PCOS), a fasting lipid panel and blood pressure measurement at diagnosis, with repeat OGTT every 1 to 3 years based on risk factors (2023 International PCOS Guideline; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Fasting glucose and HbA1c alone are sufficient screening in women under 35 with PCOS
options.rationale: Fasting glucose plus HbA1c alone is wrong because both have lower sensitivity than the 75 g OGTT for detecting impaired glucose tolerance in PCOS, where post-load hyperglycaemia is the dominant abnormality; the 2023 PCOS guideline explicitly prefers OGTT. Would be correct only when OGTT is impractical and an initial low-yield screen is accepted (2023 International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: Annual fasting insulin level to monitor for insulin resistance and guide treatment
options.rationale: Annual fasting insulin is wrong because the international guideline does not recommend fasting insulin or HOMA-IR for clinical use due to assay variability and lack of management impact; OGTT and lipid screening are the recommended tools. Would be correct only in research settings (2023 International PCOS Guideline).

options.text: Cardiac MRI with stress testing given her family history of cardiovascular disease at this age
options.rationale: Cardiac MRI plus stress testing is wrong because PCOS diagnosis does not warrant imaging-based cardiac assessment; risk stratification uses traditional calculators with intensified screening intervals. Would be correct only with symptoms or risk-calculator-driven indication for ischaemia testing (2023 International PCOS Guideline; NHMRC CV risk).

options.text: Liver biopsy because PCOS is associated with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease
options.rationale: Liver biopsy is wrong because metabolic screening at PCOS diagnosis uses non-invasive markers (LFTs, FIB-4, ultrasound) for NAFLD when suspected; biopsy is reserved for staging significant fibrosis. Would never be correct at initial diagnosis (2023 International PCOS Guideline).

explanation.summary: At PCOS diagnosis, the 2023 international guideline recommends a baseline 75 g OGTT, fasting lipid panel and BP measurement, with repeat OGTT every 1 to 3 years based on risk factors. Fasting insulin and HOMA-IR are not recommended for clinical use (2023 International PCOS Guideline; Jean Hailes).
key_points: OGTT preferred over HbA1c or fasting glucose for IGT detection in PCOS
Lipid screening recommended at diagnosis
BP, weight and waist circumference monitored yearly
Increased background risk of T2DM, GDM, dyslipidaemia, OSA, NAFLD, endometrial cancer
explanation.pearls: PCOS confers a 4-fold lifetime risk of T2DM independent of BMI; OGTT is the preferred screen.

stem: A 28-year-old nulligravid woman has been on continuous combined oral contraceptive pill (30 mcg ethinyloestradiol + levonorgestrel) plus naproxen as needed for 8 months for clinically suspected endometriosis. Her cyclical pelvic pain has not improved (still requires time off work 2 days each month with pain rated 7/10), her deep dyspareunia persists, and a repeat transvaginal ultrasound now demonstrates a 3 cm right ovarian endometrioma not previously seen. She has had no abnormal bleeding, no new bowel or bladder symptoms 2 years and She uses condoms with her long-term male partner. Beta-hCG is negative. She has no family history of breast cancer or VTE, no smoking and BMI 23. She is willing to continue medical therapy but wants more effective symptom relief; she remains reluctant to undergo surgery in the short term.

data_table.Examination: Tenderness right adnexa, no peritonism, normal speculum
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: new 3 cm right ovarian endometrioma; beta-hCG negative; CA-125 not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Switch to LNG-IUS or trial a 3-6 month GnRH analogue with add-back HRT
options.rationale: If first-line continuous COCP plus NSAID fails, second-line options include progestogen-only therapy (LNG-IUS, dienogest, DMPA) or a short course of GnRH analogue with add-back HRT to control symptoms and reduce vasomotor and bone-density side effects; this is preferable to surgery in a woman who declines it (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Stop hormonal suppression and proceed to abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral oophorectomy
options.rationale: Hysterectomy with BSO is wrong because definitive surgery in a 28-year-old with endometriosis requires completed childbearing and severe refractory symptoms after thorough medical and conservative surgical management; she has not yet had second-line therapy. Would be correct only after exhausting medical and conservative surgical options in a woman who has completed her family (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Add tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses to current therapy
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is wrong because it targets heavy menstrual bleeding rather than endometriosis-associated pain, and continuous COCP suppresses cyclical bleeding anyway. Would be correct only for HMB, not endometriosis pain (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Empirical IVF using fresh embryo transfer for presumed endometriosis-related infertility
options.rationale: Empirical IVF is wrong because IVF is for endometriosis-associated infertility (or advanced disease with planned pregnancy), not for pain management in a woman not currently planning conception. Would be correct only when fertility is the goal (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Continue current therapy and add long-acting oxycodone for breakthrough cyclical pain
options.rationale: Long-acting opioids are wrong because chronic opioid use in endometriosis pain causes dependence, hyperalgesia and no disease-modifying effect; the anti-pattern is opioid escalation rather than mechanism-targeted hormonal escalation or laparoscopic excision. Would be correct only as short-term peri-operative analgesia, never chronic (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

explanation.summary: After first-line COCP plus NSAID failure in suspected endometriosis, second-line medical options include progestogen-only therapy (LNG-IUS, dienogest, DMPA, etonogestrel implant) or a 3 to 6 month course of GnRH analogue with add-back HRT. Laparoscopic excision is offered when medical therapy fails, when fertility is impaired, or when deep infiltrating disease is suspected (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).
key_points: Endometriomas: laparoscopic cystectomy improves pain but may reduce ovarian reserve; weigh against fertility plans
GnRH analogues require add-back HRT if used >6 months to protect bone density
MRI for surgical planning of deep infiltrating endometriosis
Opioids are not recommended for chronic pelvic pain
explanation.pearls: Laparoscopic excision is preferred over ablation for deep infiltrating endometriosis.

stem: A 34-year-old G0 woman is referred with 14 months of secondary subfertility and a 9-month history of progressively heavy menstrual bleeding requiring iron supplementation (Hb 108 g/L on presentation, now 118 g/L). Her cycles are regular and ovulatory. Her partner has a normal semen analysis and her tubes are patent on HyCoSy. Transvaginal ultrasound and MRI both demonstrate a single 5 cm submucosal fibroid that is FIGO type 2 (>=50% intramural component, projecting into the endometrial cavity and distorting it) and two further small subserosal fibroids that do not distort the cavity. Her endometrial biopsy is benign. She has no other gynaecological pathology, no chronic disease, and is actively trying to conceive. She has discussed expectant management and medical options with her clinician and would prefer a definitive intervention that addresses both her heavy bleeding and improves her chance of conception.

data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L (treated iron deficiency anaemia); TVUS + MRI: 5 cm FIGO type 2 submucosal fibroid distorting cavity, two small subserosal fibroids; endometrial biopsy benign; HyCoSy bilateral patency; normal semen analysis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Hysteroscopic myomectomy as a fertility-sparing intervention addressing both HMB and subfertility
options.rationale: FIGO 0-2 submucosal fibroids distorting the cavity reduce fertility and cause HMB; hysteroscopic myomectomy is the fertility-sparing intervention of choice per RANZCOG: Fibroids and AAGL Fibroids guidance.

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system insertion as definitive treatment for both subfertility and HMB
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is wrong because cavity-distorting submucosal fibroids contraindicate Mirena placement (high expulsion, reduced efficacy) and the LNG-IUS is contraceptive rather than fertility-enhancing in a woman trying to conceive. Would be correct only for HMB without cavity distortion and without conception plans (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Uterine artery embolisation to reduce fibroid volume and HMB while preserving the uterus
options.rationale: UAE is wrong because uterine artery embolisation is not recommended for women who wish to conceive due to reduced ovarian reserve from non-target embolisation, raised placental complication risk and uncertain fertility outcome. Would be correct only for women with completed family or those declining surgery (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy as definitive management for both fibroid bulk and HMB
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is definitive for HMB and fibroid bulk but ends fertility; would be correct if the patient had completed childbearing, but this patient is actively trying to conceive per RANZCOG: Fibroids and NICE NG88.

options.text: GnRH analogue with add-back HRT as long-term medical therapy for HMB and subfertility
options.rationale: Long-term GnRH analogue is wrong because GnRH analogues induce hypogonadism and suppress ovulation; they are short-term (3-6 month) pre-operative or bridging agents and are not a fertility-preserving long-term solution. Would be correct only as pre-operative shrinkage before myomectomy (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

explanation.summary: FIGO 0 to 2 (submucosal) fibroids distorting the cavity reduce fertility and cause HMB. Hysteroscopic myomectomy is the fertility-sparing intervention of choice. UAE and hysterectomy are inappropriate when fertility is desired. LNG-IUS requires an undistorted cavity (RANZCOG: Fibroids; NICE NG88; AAGL).
key_points: FIGO 0 to 1 fibroids almost always removable hysteroscopically
FIGO 2 fibroids >=50% intramural may need staged resection
Laparoscopic or open myomectomy for intramural or subserosal causing symptoms in fertility-seeking women
Risk of caesarean delivery after deep myometrial myomectomy due to scar integrity
explanation.pearls: Always reassess cavity after hysteroscopic resection of a type 2 fibroid; second-look procedure may be needed.

stem: A 34-year-old woman has been formally diagnosed with premature ovarian insufficiency: 6 months of secondary amenorrhoea, FSH 56 IU/L and 62 IU/L on samples 5 weeks apart with low oestradiol, negative beta-hCG, normal TSH and normal prolactin. She has hot flushes, vaginal dryness with deep dyspareunia and disturbed sleep. Family history: her mother had menopause at 41 and a paternal aunt is on bisphosphonate therapy for osteoporosis. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic radiotherapy and no autoimmune disease known to date. Karyotype is pending and fragile X premutation screen, adrenal antibodies and thyroid antibodies have been ordered. She has no contraindications to oestrogen (no breast cancer, no current VTE, no migraine with aura). BMI 23. BP 118/74. She is in a long-term partnership, not currently planning pregnancy, but is open to consideration of IVF with donor oocytes in the future. A baseline DXA is planned.

data_table.Investigations: FSH 56 + 62 IU/L (4 weeks apart), low oestradiol, beta-hCG negative, normal TSH/prolactin; karyotype, FMR1, adrenal/thyroid antibodies pending; DXA planned

lead_in: What is the most appropriate hormone management strategy for bone and cardiovascular protection?

options.text: Standard-dose physiological oestradiol with cyclical progestogen or LNG-IUS until the average age of menopause
options.rationale: POI confers significantly increased risk of osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease and cognitive decline; physiological-dose HRT (oestradiol with progestogen if uterus present) until at least age 51 reduces these risks and treats vasomotor and genitourinary symptoms (ESHRE POI Guideline 2024; AMS POI; Endocrine Society).

options.text: Bisphosphonate therapy as first-line bone protection without any concurrent hormone therapy
options.rationale: Bisphosphonate first-line is wrong because POI is fundamentally an oestrogen-deficiency state; physiological HRT addresses bone, cardiovascular, vasomotor and genitourinary needs simultaneously, while bisphosphonates only target bone. Would be correct only when HRT is contraindicated or established osteoporosis fails to respond (AMS POI; ESHRE POI Guideline 2024).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill is equivalent to HRT and may be continued lifelong
options.rationale: Calling COCP equivalent to HRT lifelong is wrong because while COCP is an acceptable alternative (especially when contraception is needed - spontaneous ovulation can occur in POI), physiological HRT generally provides better bone protection; both should continue at least to age 51 rather than truly lifelong. Would be correct only with the COCP-HRT equivalence overstated and the lifelong claim modified (ESHRE POI Guideline 2024; AMS POI).

options.text: Defer all hormone therapy and review symptoms again in 6 months
options.rationale: Defer HRT 6 months is wrong because delay in oestrogen replacement accelerates bone loss, cardiovascular risk and vasomotor/urogenital symptoms; the anti-pattern is treating POI like natural menopause where 'watchful waiting' might be acceptable. Would never be correct (ESHRE POI Guideline 2024; AMS POI).

options.text: Topical vaginal oestrogen alone as the primary hormone management strategy for this woman
options.rationale: Topical vaginal oestrogen alone is wrong because vaginal oestrogen treats genitourinary symptoms only with negligible systemic absorption; it provides no bone or cardiovascular protection. Would be correct only as adjunct to systemic HRT for persistent vulvovaginal symptoms (AMS POI).

explanation.summary: POI confers significantly increased risk of osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease and cognitive decline. Physiological-dose HRT (oestradiol with progestogen if uterus present) until at least the average age of natural menopause (around 51) is the cornerstone of management. HRT is NOT contraceptive; spontaneous pregnancy occurs in ~5 to 10% (ESHRE POI Guideline 2024; AMS POI; Endocrine Society).
key_points: Diagnosis: amenorrhoea/oligomenorrhoea >=4 months + FSH >25 IU/L on 2 readings 4+ weeks apart in woman <40
Workup: karyotype, FMR1 premutation, adrenal (21-OH) and thyroid antibodies, baseline DXA
HRT options: oral or transdermal oestradiol + cyclical progestogen or LNG-IUS
Counselling: HRT is not contraceptive; spontaneous pregnancy ~5 to 10%; donor oocyte IVF main fertility pathway
explanation.pearls: Discuss contraception separately - HRT is not contraceptive.

subtopic_detail: POI - bone and cardiovascular protection plan

stem: A 37-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP for contraceptive review. She has been on a combined oral contraceptive pill (30 mcg ethinyloestradiol with levonorgestrel) for the past 4 years. At today's review her blood pressure is 162/96 mmHg, confirmed on a repeat reading 15 minutes later at 158/94 mmHg. Home BP readings over the past 2 weeks have averaged 156/94 mmHg. She has no headaches, no visual disturbancedema. Urinalysis is negative for protein and blood. She has no history of stroke, transient ischaemic attack, ischaemic heart disease, VTE or migraine with aura. She is a non-smoker, BMI 31, has otherwise normal bloods (renal function, electrolytes, lipids), and no diabetes (HbA1c 5.4%). She has completed her family and would like ongoing reliable contraception. She is reluctant to use barrier methods alone. She has no postnatal depression or mood disorder and her family history is otherwise unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 162/96, repeat 158/94, home avg 156/94; BMI 31; HR 76
data_table.Examination: Heart sounds dual, no oedema, no carotid bruit
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: no protein/blood; HbA1c 5.4%, normal lipids, renal function, electrolytes

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate contraceptive recommendation?

options.text: Continue the current combined oral contraceptive pill and re-check blood pressure in 3 months
options.rationale: Continuing COCP is wrong because systolic >=160 or diastolic >=100 is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) for combined hormonal contraception; waiting 3 months exposes her to stroke/MI risk. Would never be correct at UKMEC 4 levels (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Switch to a progestogen-only method such as the LNG-IUS, POP, etonogestrel implant or DMPA
options.rationale: Hypertension >=160/100 is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception, contraindicating COCP. Progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, LNG-IUS) are UKMEC 1 to 2 in hypertension; DMPA is UKMEC 2 in BP 140-159/90-99 and UKMEC 3 in BP >=160/100 due to its slight adverse lipid and vascular effects (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Switch to a transdermal combined hormonal patch as this avoids the first-pass hepatic metabolism
options.rationale: Switching to patch or ring is wrong because UKMEC categorises all combined hormonal methods (pill, patch, ring) the same in hypertension; the route does not modify oestrogen-driven vascular risk at this BP. Would never be correct (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Add an antihypertensive and continue the combined oral contraceptive at a lower oestrogen dose
options.rationale: Adding antihypertensive and lower-dose COCP is wrong because FSRH guidance is to avoid combined methods if BP is uncontrolled or vascular disease is present; treated hypertension is still UKMEC 3 with adequate control and UKMEC 4 if uncontrolled. Would be correct only when BP is well-controlled in low-risk younger women (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Recommend tubal ligation as the only acceptable contraceptive option for hypertensive women over 35
options.rationale: Tubal ligation as the only option is wrong because progestogen-only LARC methods (implant, LNG-IUS) are highly effective, reversible and UKMEC 1-2 in hypertension; offering sterilisation only is reductive. Would be correct only when she explicitly declines reversible methods (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

explanation.summary: Hypertension >=160/100 (uncontrolled or with vascular disease) is UKMEC category 4 for combined hormonal contraception. Progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, LNG-IUS) are UKMEC 1 to 2 and are appropriate alternatives. DMPA carries a UKMEC 3 rating at this BP level due to vascular and lipid effects (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
key_points: BP 140-159/90-99: COCP is UKMEC 3; progestogen-only methods UKMEC 1-2
BP >=160/100: COCP is UKMEC 4; progestogen-only methods UKMEC 1-2; DMPA UKMEC 3
Hypertension with established vascular disease: COCP UKMEC 4
Hypertension is the single most common cardiovascular risk encountered in contraception
explanation.pearls: UKMEC 4 means 'unacceptable health risk' - the method must not be used.

stem: A 29-year-old G0 woman with PCOS diagnosed 18 months ago (Rotterdam criteria: oligomenorrhoea, biochemical hyperandrogenism with elevated free androgen index, polycystic ovarian morphology with 26 follicles per ovary) has been trying to conceive for 12 months with intercourse 2 to 3 times per week. Her partner has a normal semen analysis on two occasions. She has bilateral patent tubes on HyCoSy. AMH 38 pmol/L, AFC 28, normal TSH and prolactin. She has lost 6 kg over 6 months with structured lifestyle intervention (current BMI 28, down from 31), and her cycles remain oligomenorrhoeic (45 to 70 days). HbA1c 5.3% and OGTT normal.

data_table.Examination: Mild hirsutism, no acanthosis nigricans, no virilisation
data_table.Investigations: AMH 38 pmol/L, AFC 28, TSH 1.6, prolactin normal, HbA1c 5.3%, OGTT normal; HyCoSy bilateral patency; partner normal semen analysis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological agent for ovulation induction in this patient?

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg daily for 5 days from cycle day 2 to 6, titrated upward if anovulatory
options.rationale: The 2023 international PCOS guideline and ASRM both recommend letrozole as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS for anovulatory infertility; letrozole achieves higher ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates than clomiphene with lower multiple pregnancy risk (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg daily for 5 days from cycle day 2 to 6 with serial follicular tracking
options.rationale: Clomiphene is wrong because PPCOS II and subsequent meta-analyses showed letrozole achieves higher ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates with lower multiple pregnancy risk; clomiphene is now second-line where letrozole is unavailable or fails. Would be correct only if letrozole is contraindicated or inaccessible (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM).

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily titrated to 1 g twice daily as sole agent for ovulation induction
options.rationale: Metformin monotherapy is wrong because it gives lower ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates than letrozole; it is an adjunct (particularly in BMI >25 or IGT) rather than primary ovulation induction. Would be correct only as adjunct to letrozole or clomiphene (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM).

options.text: Recombinant FSH gonadotrophin injection from cycle day 2 with daily follicular ultrasound monitoring
options.rationale: Injectable gonadotrophins are wrong because they are second-line after oral failure due to higher cost, OHSS risk, multiple-pregnancy risk and need for daily follicular monitoring. Would be correct only after letrozole/clomiphene resistance (2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG).

options.text: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling under general anaesthesia as a first-line surgical option for her
options.rationale: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling is wrong because it is reserved for women with clomiphene/letrozole resistance, particularly when laparoscopy is already indicated for another reason; first-line surgical treatment in treatment-naive PCOS would skip the evidence-based oral therapy. Would be correct only as second-line after oral failure (2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Letrozole is first-line ovulation induction in PCOS, achieving higher live birth rates and lower multiple pregnancy rates than clomiphene (PPCOS II trial). Metformin is an adjunct (particularly with BMI >25 or impaired glucose tolerance). Gonadotrophin injection and laparoscopic ovarian drilling are second-line (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM; RANZCOG).
key_points: Letrozole: 2.5 mg daily day 2-6; titrate to 5 mg then 7.5 mg if anovulatory
Monitor first cycle with ovulation tracking (LH urine or follicular USS)
Lifestyle intervention foundational - aim for 5 to 10% weight reduction in BMI >25
Refer to fertility specialist if anovulatory after 3 to 6 ovulatory cycles or age >=35
explanation.pearls: Letrozole is teratogenic if taken during early pregnancy; use only in early follicular phase and confirm non-pregnancy.

subtopic_detail: PCOS - ovulation induction in fertility-seeking woman

stem: A 32-year-old woman is referred to endocrinology with 14 months of secondary amenorrhoea, bilateral galactorrhoea, reduced libido and a 4-month history of headaches behind her eyes worse on waking. Two months ago she noticed difficulty seeing the side of her vision when driving. On examination today she has bitemporal hemianopia confirmed on confrontation and Humphrey perimetry. Prolactin is 9800 mIU/L (confirmed on a repeat fasting morning sample), TSH and free T4 are normal, beta-hCG negative, renal function normal and she takes no dopamine-blocking medications, antipsychotics, antidepressants or opioids. MRI pituitary demonstrates a 14 mm pituitary mass with suprasellar extension contacting the optic chiasm with no cavernous sinus invasion. Other anterior pituitary hormones (ACTH/cortisol axis, IGF-1, TSH/T4, LH/FSH) are otherwise within reference range. She is keen to preserve future fertility. She has no symptoms of pituitary apoplexy (no thunderclap headache, no collapse) and her visual loss has been gradual over months rather than days.

data_table.Examination: Bitemporal hemianopia on confrontation and Humphrey; no other focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Prolactin 9800 mIU/L (repeat fasting morning sample), TSH/T4 normal, beta-hCG negative, renal function normal; pituitary MRI: 14 mm mass with suprasellar extension contacting optic chiasm; other anterior pituitary axes intact

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly initially with close ophthalmology and endocrine follow-up
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred over bromocriptine for efficacy and tolerability) are first-line for macroprolactinomas including those with visual field deficits; tumour shrinkage and visual recovery occur in the majority within weeks to months. Surgery is reserved for failure of medical therapy or apoplexy (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011; eTG: Endocrinology).

options.text: Urgent transsphenoidal surgical resection of the pituitary mass within 48 hours
options.rationale: Urgent transsphenoidal surgery is wrong because prolactinomas, unlike other pituitary adenomas, are exquisitely sensitive to medical therapy; dopamine agonists shrink the tumour and restore vision in most cases within weeks. Would be correct only with failed/intolerant dopamine agonist therapy, pituitary apoplexy or rapidly progressive visual loss not responding to medical therapy (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: External beam radiotherapy to the pituitary fossa as primary management
options.rationale: External beam radiotherapy is wrong because radiotherapy is third-line after failed surgery or for residual aggressive disease; slow onset and risk of hypopituitarism make it unsuitable for treatment-naive prolactinoma. Would be correct only in refractory or aggressive prolactinoma after surgery (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: Observation with serial MRI and visual fields, deferring treatment until prolactin >20,000 mIU/L
options.rationale: Observation is wrong because a macroadenoma with visual field defect requires active treatment; chiasmal compression untreated causes permanent visual loss, and dopamine agonists are highly effective. Would be correct only for asymptomatic microprolactinomas with no compressive features (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: Cyclical combined oral contraceptive pill alone to restore menses and protect bone density
options.rationale: COCP alone is wrong because it treats symptomatic amenorrhoea without addressing the tumour or visual compromise, and can mask prolactin monitoring; the anti-pattern is treating the downstream symptom rather than the macroadenoma. Would be correct only for microprolactinomas with stable imaging where contraception/menstrual regulation is the priority (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

explanation.summary: Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred) are first-line for prolactinomas, including macroprolactinomas with chiasmal compression; the majority show tumour shrinkage and visual recovery within weeks to months. Transsphenoidal surgery is reserved for medical failure, intolerance, apoplexy or progressive visual loss despite treatment (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011; eTG: Endocrinology).
key_points: Cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly, titrate to prolactin response; monitor for impulse control disorder and (high cumulative dose) cardiac valve effects
Repeat MRI at 3 to 6 months to assess shrinkage; visual fields at similar interval
Pre-pregnancy planning: bromocriptine has more safety data than cabergoline in early pregnancy
Pituitary apoplexy = emergency: glucocorticoid replacement + neurosurgical referral
explanation.pearls: Don't add an SSRI or antipsychotic to a patient with new amenorrhoea/galactorrhoea without first checking prolactin and considering prolactinoma.

stem: A 28-year-old G0 woman with PCOS (AMH 48 pmol/L pre-stimulation) presents to the emergency department 9 days after oocyte retrieval (28 oocytes collected, fresh embryo transferred). She reports 3 days of progressive abdominal distension, breathlessness on minimal exertion and reduced urine output. On examination she is tachycardic (HR 112), BP 102/64, RR 24, SpO2 94% on room air, T 36.9. Abdomen is markedly distended with tense ascites and reduced bowel sounds; she has bilateral basal crackles on chest auscultation. Bloods: haematocrit 0.51, WCC 22 x10^9/L, sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 5.0 mmol/L, creatinine 124 micromol/L (baseline 60), urea 9.5 mmol/L, albumin 26 g/L, ALT 75 U/L, AST 68 U/L. CXR shows small bilateral pleural effusions. Pelvic ultrasound shows ovaries 12 cm bilaterally with large volume ascites extending above the umbilicus. Urine output has been 18 mL/hour over the last 4 hours.

data_table.Examination: Tense ascites, bilateral basal crackles, distended abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Hct 0.51, WCC 22, Na 131, K 5.0, Cr 124 (baseline 60), urea 9.5, Alb 26, ALT 75; CXR bilateral small effusions; USS ovaries 12 cm bilaterally, ascites above umbilicus; urine output 18 mL/hour; D-dimer elevated

lead_in: Which combination of interventions is the most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Admit; commence IV crystalloid with albumin, prophylactic enoxaparin, fluid balance and urgent paracentesis
options.rationale: Severe/critical OHSS with haemoconcentration (Hct >0.50), WCC >20, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise requires admission, IV crystalloid (with albumin in severe hypoalbuminaemia), VTE prophylaxis (high independent VTE risk in OHSS), strict fluid balance and paracentesis to relieve tense ascites/respiratory compromise/renal dysfunction (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Discharge home with oral fluids, simple analgesia and outpatient review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Discharge home is wrong because haemoconcentration, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise meet severe OHSS criteria that mandate inpatient IV management and close monitoring; outpatient management risks VTE, renal failure and rapid deterioration. Would be correct only for mild OHSS with adequate oral intake (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Admit and commence intravenous frusemide infusion to address oliguria and reduce the ovarian volume
options.rationale: Frusemide infusion is wrong because diuretics in an intravascularly depleted, third-spaced state worsen haemoconcentration and renal dysfunction; volume replacement must precede any diuretic consideration. Would be correct only after intravascular volume is restored and pulmonary oedema persists, not first-line in OHSS (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Urgent laparotomy with bilateral oophorectomy to remove the hyperstimulated ovaries definitively
options.rationale: Laparotomy with oophorectomy is wrong because hyperstimulated ovaries are friable and bleed readily; surgery is reserved for torsion, rupture with significant haemorrhage or ectopic pregnancy. Would be correct only for surgical complications, not for severe OHSS itself (RANZCOG OHSS).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and antiviral therapy pending blood culture results
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum antibiotics and antivirals are wrong because the elevated WCC in OHSS reflects the inflammatory state rather than infection; empirical antibiotics are not indicated without clinical evidence of infection. Would be correct only when superimposed infection is clinically suspected (RANZCOG OHSS).

explanation.summary: Severe/critical OHSS with haemoconcentration, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise requires admission, judicious IV crystalloid (albumin if albumin <30 g/L), VTE prophylaxis (OHSS confers independent high VTE risk), strict fluid balance and paracentesis for tense ascites or respiratory/renal compromise. Diuretics are contraindicated until intravascular volume is restored (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).
key_points: Critical OHSS thresholds: Hct >0.55, WCC >25, tense ascites, ARDS, VTE, hepatorenal dysfunction, anuria
Severe OHSS thresholds: Hct >0.45, WCC >15, clinical ascites, oliguria, hypoalbuminaemia <35 g/L
VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin throughout admission and at least until clinical resolution; longer if pregnancy ensues
Continued worsening if pregnancy occurs (HCG-driven) - persistent symptoms warrant beta-hCG check
explanation.pearls: OHSS is one of the few conditions where IV albumin is routinely used; diuretics are explicitly contraindicated until intravascular volume is restored.

stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP for contraceptive review. She was diagnosed 14 months ago with stage II oestrogen-receptor-positive, progesterone-receptor-positive, HER2-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast. She had wide local excision plus axillary node sampling (1 of 3 nodes positive), adjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy, and commenced tamoxifen 20 mg daily 6 months ago which she plans to continue for at least 5 to 10 years. She has been amenorrhoeic since chemotherapy but recent bloods show a partial return of menses and an FSH of 18 IU/L with oestradiol 280 pmol/L, suggesting recovery of ovarian function. She uses condoms intermittently with her long-term male partner. She has completed her family. She is anxious to avoid pregnancy because she does not wish to interrupt her endocrine therapy and is aware of the teratogenicity of tamoxifen. She has no other major comorbidity. BMI 24, BP 118/74.

data_table.Investigations: FSH 18 IU/L, E2 280 pmol/L (recovering ovarian function); on tamoxifen 20 mg daily

lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Copper intrauterine device (Cu-IUD) inserted at the next available family planning visit
options.rationale: Current and past breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for ALL hormonal contraception (combined and progestogen-only). The copper IUD is UKMEC 1 and is the recommended highly effective reversible option in a woman with breast cancer who has completed her family; female sterilisation is also acceptable (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena) inserted at the next available appointment
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is wrong because current breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for the LNG-IUS; despite local progestogen action, systemic exposure means it is contraindicated in current/recent breast cancer (UKMEC 3 only when remission is >5 years). Would be correct only after >5 years' disease-free remission and only as UKMEC 3 (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill at the lowest effective oestrogen dose available
options.rationale: COCP is wrong because current/recent breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception due to oestrogen-driven recurrence risk; absolute contraindication. Would never be correct in current/recent breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal contraceptive implant (Implanon NXT) for 3 years
options.rationale: Etonogestrel implant is wrong because UKMEC 4 applies to all progestogen-only methods (implant, POP, DMPA, LNG-IUS) in current breast cancer; the systemic progestogen exposure is unacceptable. Would never be correct in current breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg IM every 12 weeks
options.rationale: DMPA is wrong because depot progestogen is UKMEC 4 in current breast cancer; all hormonal methods are contraindicated. Would never be correct in current breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

explanation.summary: Current breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for ALL hormonal contraception (combined and progestogen-only, including LNG-IUS). The copper IUD is UKMEC 1 and is the recommended highly effective reversible option. Female sterilisation is also acceptable (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).
key_points: Current breast cancer (any subtype, any time on treatment): UKMEC 4 for all hormonal methods
Past breast cancer (>5 years remission): UKMEC 3 for hormonal methods
Copper IUD: UKMEC 1 in current and past breast cancer
Tamoxifen is teratogenic - contraception is mandatory throughout treatment and for 2 months after
explanation.pearls: Don't be reassured by the 'local action' of the LNG-IUS in current breast cancer - it is still UKMEC 4.

stem: A 31-year-old G1P1 woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (diagnosed 6 years ago) attends for contraceptive review. Her disease has been clinically and serologically stable for 18 months on hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisolone (5 mg daily). She has previously had two episodes of cutaneous lupus and one episode of mild lupus nephritis treated with mycophenolate, now in remission. Her most recent immunology shows positive lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 12 weeks apart, anticardiolipin IgG strongly positive, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I positive (triple positive antiphospholipid profile). She has no personal history of venous thromboembolism, stroke or ischaemic heart disease and no obstetric morbidity but is high risk for VTE. BP 120/76, BMI 25, non-smoker. She has completed her family, uses condoms inconsistently and would like a more reliable method. She is concerned about both medication interactions with hydroxychloroquine and the impact of contraception on her lupus and VTE risk.

data_table.Examination: No active rash, no active arthritis
data_table.Investigations: Lupus anticoagulant positive x2 (12 weeks apart), anticardiolipin IgG strongly positive, anti-beta-2-GPI positive; complement and dsDNA stable; renal function normal

lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill with the lowest available oestrogen dose to minimise VTE risk
options.rationale: COCP is wrong because SLE with positive (or unknown) antiphospholipid antibodies is UKMEC 4 for all combined hormonal contraception due to substantially elevated VTE and arterial thrombosis risk; lowering the oestrogen dose does not change the category. Would never be correct with APL positive (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) inserted at the next available appointment
options.rationale: In SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies, the LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1 to 2: highly effective, no systemic oestrogen, minimal interaction with hydroxychloroquine, low VTE risk, and acceptable in a woman who has completed her family. The Cu-IUD (UKMEC 1) is also acceptable but the LNG-IUS additionally reduces menstrual blood loss (relevant if anticoagulation is initiated later) (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Combined hormonal contraceptive vaginal ring as it avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism
options.rationale: Combined ring is wrong because UKMEC 4 applies to all combined methods regardless of route in APL-positive SLE; route does not modify thrombotic risk. Would never be correct in this group (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg IM every 12 weeks for ongoing contraception
options.rationale: DMPA is wrong because it is UKMEC 3 in APL-positive SLE (adverse lipid profile and small but increased VTE association), so an IUD is preferred. Would be correct only when LARC IUDs are not feasible and benefits are judged to outweigh risks (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Continue barrier contraception alone (condoms) given the complexity of medication interactions
options.rationale: Condoms alone is wrong because typical-use failure ~13%/year is inadequate when she has completed her family and seeks reliable contraception; effective LARC is available and appropriate. Would be correct only when she explicitly preferred barrier methods after counselling (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

explanation.summary: SLE with positive (or unknown) antiphospholipid antibodies is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception. The LNG-IUS (UKMEC 1-2) and copper IUD (UKMEC 1) are first-line LARC options. DMPA is UKMEC 3 due to lipid and small VTE association. Progestogen-only pill and implant are UKMEC 3 (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).
key_points: Triple-positive antiphospholipid profile carries highest thrombosis risk
Stable SLE without antiphospholipid antibodies: COCP UKMEC 2
Pregnancy planning in antiphospholipid syndrome requires multidisciplinary management with aspirin +/- LMWH
Hydroxychloroquine is safe in pregnancy and reduces lupus flares and obstetric APS complications
explanation.pearls: Always check antiphospholipid antibody status before prescribing combined hormonal contraception in any woman with SLE.

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends for contraceptive review 7 months after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery. She has lost 28 kg (BMI now 32, down from 42 pre-operatively). She has not yet resumed menses since surgery and reports breast tenderness, fatigue and intermittent nausea, which she attributes to her vitamin and mineral supplementation. A urine beta-hCG today is negative. Her past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily (BP today 128/78) and previous gestational diabetes (no current diabetes; HbA1c 5.5%). She uses condoms inconsistently with her long-term male partner. Pre-operatively she was on the combined oral contraceptive pill which was stopped 6 weeks before surgery as part of the VTE risk minimisation protocol. She is fully aware that bariatric surgery is associated with rapid return of fertility and that pregnancy is recommended to be deferred for 12 to 24 months post-operatively. She wishes to use a highly effective method until she is ready for a third pregnancy in 18 months.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena), copper IUD or etonogestrel subdermal implant
options.rationale: After malabsorptive bariatric procedures (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, biliopancreatic diversion), oral hormonal contraceptive absorption is unreliable; LARCs that bypass the gut (LNG-IUS, Cu-IUD, etonogestrel implant) are preferred. The COCP also carries elevated VTE risk in the post-operative period and may be UKMEC 3 to 4 with persistent obesity and other risk factors; pregnancy should be delayed 12 to 24 months post-bariatric surgery (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill at standard dose with continued condom backup
options.rationale: COCP after RYGB is wrong because the malabsorptive bypass impairs absorption of oral hormonal contraception, making efficacy unreliable regardless of condom backup; FSRH advises against oral methods after malabsorptive bariatric surgery. Would be correct only after restrictive procedures (sleeve gastrectomy, band) where absorption is preserved (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill taken daily with a 3-hour mistiming window
options.rationale: POP is wrong because, like the COCP, POP absorption is impaired after Roux-en-Y; the narrow timing window worsens the reliability problem. Would be correct only after restrictive procedures (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Fertility awareness method timed to predicted ovulation each cycle
options.rationale: Fertility awareness is wrong because typical-use failure ~24%/year is incompatible with the need for reliable contraception, and post-bariatric menstrual irregularity makes cycle tracking unreliable. Would be correct only as personal preference after counselling about high failure rates (Therapeutic Guidelines: Contraception).

options.text: Recommend immediate pregnancy attempts given strong weight loss and improving metabolic profile
options.rationale: Immediate pregnancy attempts is wrong because ASMBS/ACOG and RANZCOG recommend deferring pregnancy 12-24 months post-bariatric surgery to allow weight stabilisation, micronutrient optimisation (B12, iron, folate, vitamin D) and reduce SGA, prematurity and nutritional deficiency in the foetus. Would be correct only after the 12-24 month interval and nutritional optimisation (ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: After malabsorptive bariatric procedures (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, biliopancreatic diversion), oral hormonal contraception has reduced and unreliable absorption. LARCs that bypass the gut (LNG-IUS, Cu-IUD, etonogestrel implant, DMPA injection) are preferred. Restrictive procedures (sleeve gastrectomy, gastric band) do not impair absorption to the same degree. Pregnancy should be delayed 12 to 24 months post-bariatric surgery (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy; RANZCOG).
key_points: Malabsorptive procedures (RYGB, BPD): avoid oral hormonal contraception
Restrictive procedures (sleeve, band): oral methods acceptable if absorbed
Rapid return of fertility post-weight loss; high unintended pregnancy risk
Multidisciplinary pre-conception planning: micronutrient repletion, optimised glycaemia, mental health
explanation.pearls: After Roux-en-Y, treat oral hormonal contraception as you would an enzyme-inducing medication - assume unreliable absorption.
